div.head: The Minister in Haiti (Armour) to the Secretary of State
div.p: 1. For Welles.Sumner Welles, Assistant Secretary of State. PixleyRex A. Pixley, Deputy Fiscal Representative of the Haitian Government. and I have had further talks with HibbertLucien Hibbert, Haitian Minister for Foreign Affairs. and I have conveyed to him the contents of the Department’s telegram No. 100, December 31, 3 p.m.Foreign Relations, 1934, vol. v, p. 332.

He informs us that BlanchetAlbert Blanchet, Haitian Minister in the United States. is being instructed to assure you and the Secretary that the Haitian Government has no intention of repudiating or doing anything to reverse the Montevideo commitments.Resolution V, Economic, Commercial, and Tariff Policy, approved December 16, 1933, Report of the Delegates of the United States of America to the Seventh International Conference of American States, Montevideo, Uruguay, December 3–26, 1933 (Washington, 1934), pp. 196–198. His position is based solely on loss of revenue which he feels would endanger the budget. Furthermore, the French and Italians who take the bulk of Haitian coffee have notified the Haitian Government of their intention to negotiate new treaties and Hibbert fears that if reductions proposed by the United States which takes so little from Haiti are accepted aside from losses mentioned it would serve as a dangerous precedent for these countries which are not bound by Montevideo commitments and which are frankly advocates of the “donnant donnant” principle.
